data.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Motivation</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>The COVID-19 pandemic has ignited a broad scientific interest in viral research in general and coronavirus research in particular. The identification and characterization of viral species in natural reservoirs typically involves de novo assembly. However, existing genome, metagenome and transcriptome assemblers often are not able to assemble many viruses (including coronaviruses) into a single contig. Coverage variation between datasets and within dataset, presence of close strains, splice variants and contamination set a high bar for assemblers to process viral datasets with diverse properties.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>We developed coronaSPAdes, a novel assembler for RNA viral species recovery in general and coronaviruses in particular. coronaSPAdes leverages the knowledge about viral genome structures to improve assembly extending ideas initially implemented in biosyntheticSPAdes. We have shown that coronaSPAdes outperforms existing SPAdes modes and other popular short-read metagenome and viral assemblers in the recovery of full-length RNA viral genomes.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Availability and implementation</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>coronaSPAdes version used in this article is a part of SPAdes 3.15 release and is freely available at http://cab.spbu.ru/software/spades.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Supplementary information</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>Supplementary data are available at Bioinformatics online.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
title: coronaSPAdes: from biosynthetic gene clusters to RNA viral assemblies

<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Motivation</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    The COVID-19 pandemic has ignited a broad scientific interest in viral research in general and coronavirus research in particular. The identification and characterization of viral species in natural reservoirs typically involves
                    <jats:italic>de novo</jats:italic>
                    assembly. However, existing genome, metagenome and transcriptome assemblers often are not able to assemble many viruses (including coronaviruses) into a single contig. Coverage variation between datasets and within dataset, presence of close strains, splice variants and contamination set a high bar for assemblers to process viral datasets with diverse properties.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We developed coronaSPAdes, a novel assembler for RNA viral species recovery in general and coronaviruses in particular. coronaSPAdes leverages the knowledge about viral genome structures to improve assembly extending ideas initially implemented in biosyntheticSPAdes. We have shown that coronaSPAdes outperforms existing SPAdes modes and other popular short-read metagenome and viral assemblers in the recovery of full-length RNA viral genomes.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Availability</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    coronaSPAdes version used in this article is a part of SPAdes 3.15 release and is freely available at
                    <jats:ext-link xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" ext-link-type="uri" xlink:href="http://cab.spbu.ru/software/spades">http://cab.spbu.ru/software/spades</jats:ext-link>
                    .

data.abstract: <jats:p><strong> 7–8 September 2026 | Sheffield, UK &amp; online (co-located with RSECon26) </strong> Join a global gathering of leaders and change-makers advancing strategic coordination, sustainability, and collaboration across the research software community. IRSC fosters global cohesion, community connection, and strategic impact to strengthen research software as a vital part of research and innovation.</jats:p>
title: International Research Software Conference

Photo credit: Annelies Verhelst November, 2022 Authors: Michelle Barker, Valentina Azzarà, Tom Bakker, Lieke de Boer, Neil Chue Hong, Maria Cruz, Niels Drost, Joris van Eijnatten, Maaike de Jong, Daniel S. Katz, Carlos Martinez-Ortiz, Veronica Pang This blog post is being cross-posted by the Research Software Alliance and Netherlands eScience Center.</jats:p>
title: Global gathering of research software funders sets the agenda for supporting sustainable research software

data.abstract: <jats:p>By Michelle Barker, Chris Erdmann, Eric Jensen, Kim Hartley, Anna Henriksson, Suné Joubert October 2024 The 2024 International Research Software Funders Workshop continued to progress the way funders sustain and support research software.</jats:p>
title: International Research Software Funders Workshop 2024 – Building a Sustainable Future for Research Software
Being a Software Engineer in the Software Century

data.abstract: <jats:p><em> September, 2024 </em> Authors: Michelle Barker, Wilhelm Hasselbring Directions for Research Software Engineering Research RSE research is a new direction in the evolving field of Research Software Engineering (RSE). This concept was highlighted in a panel chaired by Wilhelm Hasselbring (Kiel University) at RSECon in September 2024.</jats:p>
title: Directions for Research Software Engineering Research

data.abstract: <jats:p><em> Cross-posted by Better Scientific Software (BSSw) and ReSA </em> . How can we reframe research software engineering (RSE) to place diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) as a central organising principle in the community?</jats:p>
title: Vive la différence - research software engineers
